blocks.content: The gallery was between a dry cleaner and a hardware store, which Ethan had always found quietly perfect.

The sign above the door was small—Halsted Works—and the interior was narrow and long, walls hung with pieces from artists Ethan had admired for years. The owner, a woman named June who wore paint-stained overalls and read during slow hours, looked up when they walked in and nodded once at Ethan the way people nod at regulars.
Cassandra turned in a slow circle just inside the door.
Softly. Like the word had come out before she decided to say it.

She moved the way she always moved—with her whole body, not just her eyes. She stopped at the first piece: a large canvas, abstract, deep blues and grey-greens folding into each other like weather seen from above.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Something trying to become something else.
She said it immediately. He hadn't expected that.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: The blues are fighting the grey. But not angrily. It's more like—
Ethan looked at the painting again.
He had stood in front of it many times.
He had never thought of it as negotiating.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: That read. Most people just say it's pretty.
She shrugged, but her eyes were light.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I spend a lot of time thinking about things that are trying to coexist.

They moved deeper into the gallery. Ethan found himself talking—quietly, carefully, the way he never talked to most people. About the brushwork on a small portrait near the back. About the artist, a woman from this city who had gone almost entirely unnoticed until two years ago. About the specific shade of amber in a landscape near the window that he had been trying to recreate for six months without success.
She asked questions that proved she had heard him.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Does it bother you? That some of it doesn't get seen?

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: Because it was made to be seen.

blocks.speaker: Ethan
blocks.content: The artist put something real into it. And it's just sitting here. Most people walk past. They don't know what they're walking past.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You paint like that too, don't you? Things you don't think people will stop for.
She looked back at the painting.
